Criterion used in Juneau et al. (2023): Yes if the described study group consisted of >90% of eligible individuals
Earlier wording in Mulder et al. (2019): Yes if the described study group consisted of >90% of the original cohort of childhood cancer survivors, or was a random sample with respect to cancer treatment
Adaptation made in Juneau et al. (2023): Generalised from a childhood-cancer cohort frame to a broader eligible-population frame; the explicit random-sample clause was not carried over

Criterion used in Juneau et al. (2023): Yes if the intervention and number of participants was defined
Earlier wording in Mulder et al. (2019): Yes if the type of chemotherapy and/or location of radiotherapy was mentioned and the number of participants with chronic viral hepatitis was mentioned
Adaptation made in Juneau et al. (2023): Generalised from cancer-treatment exposure and hepatitis-status reporting to a broader intervention-and-sample-description criterion

Criterion used in Juneau et al. (2023): Yes if the outcome was assessed for at least 60% of the study group of interest
Earlier wording in Mulder et al. (2019): Yes if the outcome was assessed for >90% of the study group of interest (++) or for 60% to 90% of the study group of interest (+)
Adaptation made in Juneau et al. (2023): Collapsed Mulder's two favourable follow-up thresholds into one broader yes condition at ≥60%

Criterion used in Juneau et al. (2023): Yes if the length of follow-up was mentioned
Earlier wording in Mulder et al. (2019): Yes if the length of follow-up was mentioned

Criterion used in Juneau et al. (2023): Yes if the outcome assessors were blinded to the investigated determinant
Earlier wording in Mulder et al. (2019): Yes if the outcome assessors were blinded to the investigated determinant; Mulder added that if outcomes were biochemical measurements produced by a machine, blinding of the investigator was not relevant
Adaptation made in Juneau et al. (2023): Kept the general blinding criterion but removed the liver-biochemistry-specific caveat from the criterion wording

Criterion used in Juneau et al. (2023): Yes if the outcome definition was objective and precise
Earlier wording in Mulder et al. (2019): Yes if the outcome definition was objective and precise, i.e., if the upper limits of normal for liver function tests were described in the definition of hepatic late adverse effects
Adaptation made in Juneau et al. (2023): Generalised from a liver-test-specific definition to a broader outcome-definition criterion

Criterion used in Juneau et al. (2023): Yes if important prognostic factors (i.e. age, gender) or follow-up were taken adequately into account
Earlier wording in Mulder et al. (2019): Yes if important prognostic factors (i.e. age, gender, co-treatment) or follow-up were taken adequately into account
Adaptation made in Juneau et al. (2023): Retained the same structure but removed the cancer-specific example of co-treatment

Criterion used in Juneau et al. (2023): Yes if the method of analysis was described and the effect of the intervention was quantified
Earlier wording in Mulder et al. (2019): Yes if a relative risk, odds ratio, attributable risk, linear or logistic regression model, mean difference, or Chi² was calculated
Adaptation made in Juneau et al. (2023): Generalised from a list of named statistical measures to a broader requirement that the analysis be described and the effect quantified
